question_title: Will the interest in “federal reserve chair jerome powell” change between 2026-03-16 and 2026-03-28 according to Google Trends?
summary:
The search term "federal reserve chair jerome powell" has a baseline of 0-2 on Google Trends. The starting value on 2026-03-16 in the resolution date range is 3, following a massive spike to 100 on March 14 from the court ruling quashing DOJ subpoenas against Powell, which has already decayed rapidly.

**Decreases is virtually impossible**: Starting at 3, the minimum Google Trends value is 0, so the maximum decline is -3. The resolution criterion requires "more than 3 lower," which cannot be met.

**"Doesn't change" is strongly favored**: The [0, 6] band captures nearly all normal fluctuation for this term. Historical base rates show 83-92% no-change rates. The FOMC meeting on March 17-18 will generate a spike, but based on the January precedent (peaked at 7, faded in 5 days), it would fully dissipate by March 28 (10 days later). No major scheduled events near March 28.

**"Increases" has a meaningful but minority probability (~18%)**: Driven by the possibility that the ongoing legal drama (DOJ appeal of subpoena ruling), Trump's pressure on Powell, Kevin Warsh confirmation process, or other unexpected events could produce a newsworthy spike specifically near March 28 that pushes the value to 7+.

factors.description: Decreases is mathematically impossible: starting value is 3, Google Trends minimum is 0, so max decrease is -3 which does NOT meet the 'more than 3 lower' threshold
Base rate strongly favors no change: 3-month change_stats show 92.2% no-change rate, resolution-range shows 83.3%. This specific search term normally sits at 0-2 (baseline).
Starting value of 3 is already slightly elevated (post-spike residual from Mar 14 court ruling). Most likely to decay back to 0-1, well within the 'Doesn't change' band of [0,6].

FOMC meeting March 17-18 with Powell press conference is a known catalyst. Jan FOMC meeting produced a value of 7 on the spike day (Jan 30). However, March 28 is 10 days after FOMC — the Jan spike faded to baseline within 5 days.

Active legal drama: DOJ appealing subpoena ruling, Trump continuing to pressure Powell, Warsh nomination pending, Powell's term ending May 15. Any of these could produce a spike near March 28, but timing is unpredictable.
Powell speech on March 21 (ASPA virtual award acceptance) is minor and unlikely to generate significant search interest for this long-tail term. No major scheduled event near March 28.

For 'Increases' to resolve, value on March 28 must be 7+. Historical data shows this term reaches 7+ only during major spikes (court ruling=100, FOMC day=7). Probability of such a spike on the specific date of March 28 is low.

reasoning:
**Forecast: "Doesn't change" at 80%, "Increases" at 18%, "Decreases" at 2%**

Key reasoning:

1. **Decreases is essentially impossible** — starting at 3, the value can only drop to 0 (a change of -3), which doesn't meet the "more than 3 lower" threshold.

2. **"Doesn't change" dominates** — the [0, 6] band is wide relative to this term's normal range of 0-2. The FOMC meeting spike on March 18 will fade well before March 28 based on the January precedent (spike to 7, back to baseline in 5 days). No scheduled events near March 28.

3. **18% for "Increases"** — accounts for the ongoing Powell legal drama (DOJ appeal), Trump pressure, and Warsh nomination potentially generating an unscheduled news spike close to March 28 that pushes the value to 7+.

resolution_reason: The Google Trends data shows the value for "federal reserve chair jerome powell" on 2026-03-16 was 3 and on 2026-03-28 was 0. The difference is -3, meaning the value decreased by exactly 3 points. According to the resolution criteria, the question resolves "Decreases" only if the value is "more than 3 lower" - since it is exactly 3 lower (not more than 3), this falls within the "Doesn't change" category (within 3 of the original value). Note: There is slight uncertainty because the fine print mentions the API results may differ from what appears on the page, and my tool access may not be identical to the SerpAPI script that will officially resolve this.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves “Doesn't change” if the value on the timeline at [https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=federal%20reserve%20chair%20jerome%20powell&date=2026-02-26%202026-03-28](https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=federal%20reserve%20chair%20jerome%20powell&date=2026-02-26%202026-03-28) for 2026-03-28 is within 3 of the value at 2026-03-16. It resolves “Increases” if the value is more than 3 greater, and resolves “Decreases” if the value is more than 3 lower.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will access the data on Google Trends using [SerpApi](https://serpapi.com/), and compare the height of the timeline for the two aforementioned dates. The specific python query will be structured as follows:
```
from serpapi import GoogleSearch

search = GoogleSearch(params={
  "api_key": API_KEY,
  "engine": "google_trends",
  "data_type": "TIMESERIES",
  "geo": "US",
  "tz": 0,
  "q": "federal reserve chair jerome powell",
  "date": "2026-02-26 2026-03-28",
})
search.get_dict()
```
Note that there may be differences between the results returned by the API and the data appearing on the page. This seems to be due to the 'tz' parameter not having the intended effect in-browser. In this case, the API results will be considered authoritative.
Note that the precision of the timeline will be 1 day, so this will compare the overall interest for the whole day as determined by Google Trends.
Dates are determined in UTC.
If the data is no longer available, or the script fails, this question will be annulled or manually resolved by a moderator.
